relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: YHWH speaks cities, land, and refuge in the plains of Moab.
Levites receive places to live within the inheritances of others.
Cities and pasture support households, animals, and service.
Space around each city is measured for common life.
The city stands at the center of an ordered pasture boundary.
Six of the forty-eight cities become places of refuge.
Levite life is distributed through forty-eight cities.
Communities give according to the size of what they received.
YHWH speaks the interruption of blood vengeance.
The refuge law begins at the threshold of the land.
A person who kills unintentionally may flee before revenge reaches them.
Refuge creates time for judgment instead of immediate death.
Six cities form the sheltering network.
Refuge stands on both sides of Jordan.
Israelite, sojourner, and resident settler share the same protection.
The law then names lethal intent through an iron weapon.
A deadly stone may reveal murderous action.
A deadly wooden instrument may do the same.
Ancient blood vengeance remains embedded in the law.
Hatred and ambush distinguish murder from accident.
Enmity and deliberate striking carry the text's severest judgment.
Sudden harm without hatred is treated differently.
Unseen accident without intent enters the refuge path.
The congregation must judge between accusation and circumstance.
The community rescues the unintentional killer and restores them to refuge.
Leaving the refuge boundary removes its protection in the ancient system.
The blood-avenger may kill outside that boundary without liability.
The high priest's death finally releases the protected exile.
The refuge structure becomes an enduring judgment statute.
No death sentence may rest on one witness alone.
Wealth cannot purchase exemption for intentional murder.
Nor can it purchase early release from refuge.
Shed blood is pictured as a wound in the land itself.
The land must not be defiled because YHWH dwells among the people.

contemplative_note: Numbers 35 contains both refuge and revenge. Its life-giving trajectory is the creation of distance, time, evidence, intent analysis, and equal asylum before anger becomes irreversible violence. Modern reception must carry that trajectory beyond execution toward protection and nonlethal justice.
